people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Aunt Ellen; Father; Theodore

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Father; Aunt Ally

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Aunt Ally; Father; Aunt Gaillard
notes: Continuation of the handwritten account of Grandmother's last illness. Continues onto subsequent pages.

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Cousin Esther; Cousin Robert Gourdin; Cousin Theodore Gourdin; Father; Rev. Mr. Drayton

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Rev. Mr. Drayton; Cousin Esther; Father

Mr. Drayton's words of comfort, her response to subsequent visits, her reference back to the 'Dark River' allegory, and the dated entry for Sunday 29th noting her continuing decline. Continues onto the next page.

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Rev. Mr. Drayton

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Joseph Stafford Barker; Samuel Barker; Ellen Milliken

Second and concluding page of the handwritten poem begun on page 055. Cursive in dark ink on the verso of the folded sheet. The final stanza closes the poem with the picket's death. The last line 'And the life blood' appears with a struck-through preceding phrase.

people: Ellen; Theos

A small pencilled annotation near the salutation reads 'Two Sons of Paul / & Two daughters / of [Mrs?] Walker [uncertain]'. Letter continues onto the next page.

Page 2 of the handwritten letter begun on page 057. The writer continues the meditation on Christian duty, expanding on benevolence, almsgiving, and the example of Christ. The page breaks mid-sentence; the letter continues onto subsequent pages.

A handwritten letter (or letter fragment) on Christian almsgiving and benevolence, written in faded brown ink on a folded sheet. The hand is small and tightly spaced; ink bleed and creases make many words difficult. Continues onto page 060. Author and recipient not identified on this page.

Conclusion of the philosophical letter on Christian almsgiving begun on page 059. The closing line at the bottom of the sheet appears to bear a signature in pencilled annotation, very faint and partly obscured by an ink blot.

people: Ellen Milliken; Susan Milliken Barker; Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Sanford; Mr. Owens

A circular embossed seal is impressed at upper left. The letter continues onto pages 062 and 063.

people: Mother; Brother; Uncle Eddie

Continues onto subsequent pages.

people: Albert Sidney Johnston

people: Albert Sidney Johnston

people: Albert Sidney Johnston

people: Susan Milliken Barker; Stevens

people: Susan Milliken Barker; Lydia C. Bullard

Cross-writing makes most of the text very difficult to decipher; only fragments are confidently legible. A travel narrative — boats, banks, a sick child carried by a 'white girl', heat, a wagon ride from a steamer landing — runs through the readable portions.

The second sheet of the cross-hatched (over-written at right angles) handwritten letter begun on page 071. Cross-writing makes the page largely illegible; only scattered phrases can be read with confidence.

people: Mrs. Frank Hampton; Mama; Mr. Wade; Singleton; Mr. Wade; Pape; Brother

people: Mrs. Walpole; Peter; Martha; Kate Stoney

Civil War-era references to 'Yankees' appear on the next page.

people: Peter; Clem

The writer describes how 'Peter' (a servant) had stolen shirts from her trunks, claiming the Yankees had distributed them, and explains why she did not confront him. She closes as 'Clem' wakes up. The reference to Yankees places the letter during or just after the Civil War.

people: Hugh Miller; Mr. Kingsley

The argument moves from the literary 'Voice' to the dignity of the home and the daily work of women, before turning to a moral about teaching. Several lines at the top edge and bottom edge are partly lost or illegible.

Page 1 of a handwritten letter beginning 'My Dear Sir,' written in brown ink in a neat sloping hand. The writer thanks his correspondent for a letter pointing out something about Shelley's 'Prometheus,' praises 'Prometheus Unbound' as one of the most remarkable productions in any language, and recalls being puzzled in boyhood by the reflection of Christ in the Saturnian Titan. Letter continues onto the next page.

Page 2 of the multi-page handwritten letter begun on page 078, in the same brown ink and sloping hand. The writer continues his discussion of Shelley and the figure of Christ, and turns to Aeschylus. Letter continues onto the next page.

Page 3 of the multi-page handwritten letter begun on page 078, in the same brown ink and sloping hand. The writer continues his comparison of Shelley with Aeschylus and reflects on Shelley's scepticism. A vertical fold runs down the centre of the page. Letter continues onto the next page.

people: Susan Milliken Barker
notes: Concluding page of the multi-page handwritten letter begun on page 078. Miles' and the addressee at lower left is 'Samuel G. Barker Esqr.' A vertical fold runs down the centre of the page.

people: Susan Milliken Barker

The writer reports forwarding war news by telegraph, describes a position two miles from a river opposite enemy forces protected by a creek and gun boats, reflects on Providence and Mr. Pillow, and praises the troops' resilience. Large portions of the sheet are torn away; only edge fragments remain at right and bottom. Letter continues onto the next scan.

Coleman, Frank Campbell, and Pomerie.

Names and place references that can be glimpsed include Saunders, Naughty, Steven, Mary, Lynch, Greenville, Barber, Wheeler, Brazian, Henry, Sirona, Friday, Thursday, Davis, Yankees, Pat Sumter.

The writer recounts a buggy outing, a funeral, household details about cooking and the children's health, and a disappointing Sunday sermon by a German preacher at the Methodist church. Several spots and stains in the upper portion of the sheet. The page ends mid-sentence and continues onto the next scan.

people: William

The northern counties are shaded orange and the southern counties green. Date line at top reads 'SUNDAY, MARCH 13, [year illegible].' A small caption box at the lower right gives a few sentences of explanatory text below a small inset showing two figures and a globe. A typewritten sheet headed 'FITZ SIMONS' giving heraldic descriptions of the family arms, crest, colors, and the symbolism of the various heraldic elements (label, fesse, crescent, boar). The sheet is held to the album page at the top by a paperclip and at the bottom by what appears to be a slip of underlying paper. Sources cited at the head are Fox-Davies, Fairbairn, and at the foot Hobbies Magazine.

people: Christopher FitzSimons (the emigrant)

The sheet describes the family arms and crest, glosses 'Fitz' as 'Son', and gives the meanings of the heraldic colors and charges (Sable, Argent, Label, Fesse, Crescent, Boar). A closing note attributes the meanings of Label, Fesse, Crescent, and Boar to 'Hobbies Magazine.'

A printed and hand-colored heraldic illustration centered on the page. The shield bears three crescents on a sable field with a silver fesse (the central crescent on the silver band, and one crescent in chief at left, one in chief at right, and one in base). Above the shield is a tilted helm with green and silver mantling, surmounted by an eagle crest, and above the eagle a small radiant sun with a face. Below the shield, in blackletter type: 'Fitz Simons.'

A black-and-white ink drawing of a Fitz Simons coat of arms, mounted on the album page. The shield, with serrated edges and dark shading, bears two crescents in chief, a fesse (charged with what appears to be a label or band) across the middle, and a crescent in base. Above the shield is a wreath topped by a small boar (passant). Decorative mantling spreads to either side and an empty banderole curves across the base. Below the shield, in blackletter: 'Fitz Simons.'

notes: A typewritten page on lightly aged paper, the opening of a legal complaint in the Court of Common Pleas, State of South Carolina, Berkeley County. Caption identifies Theodore G. Barker, Henrietta Catherine Barker, and Joe Joiner, Defendants. Paragraphs 1 through 4 recount: that Plaintiff is the qualified Executor of John B. Milliken; that the named Defendants (excepting Joe Joiner) are the surviving children of Mrs. Ellen Barker, widow of Samuel G. Barker, and sister of John B. Milliken; that Edward P. Milliken (brother of John B. Milliken) died about Nov. 4, 1877 leaving his Last Will and Testament; and that John B. Milliken at his death was seized of a tract of land in Berkeley County known as Castle Hugh Tract, lying between the Northeastern Railroad Track and a Pineland Settlement, with Joe Joiner living thereon under an agreement for purchase. Two stamped marginal notes appear at upper right. Continues onto page 173.

notes: A typewritten page continuing the legal complaint of Theodore G. Barker (Plaintiff) against Susan M. FitzSimons et al. (Defendants). Paragraphs 5 through 8 recount: that Susan Milliken (sister and devisee of Edward P. Milliken) departed this life on May 3, 1883, leaving as her heirs at law and distributees her brother John B. Milliken and the children of her deceased sister Ellen Barker; that John B. Milliken died on Nov. 1, 1889 leaving his Last Will and Testament dated Feb. 24, 1882; that the only heirs at law and distributees of John B. Milliken are the children of his deceased sister Ellen Barker; and that at his death the Real Estate of John B. Milliken consisted of (a) a tract of land in Williamsburg County between the Lanes and Salters stations on the Northeastern Railroad (containing 178 acres more or less), (b) a one-half interest in the Castle Hugh Tract, and (c) a Plantation tract of land on Cooper River known as North Mulberry. Several handwritten ink insertions appear in the margins clarifying conveyance of property. Continues from page 172 onto page 174.

notes: First page of a handwritten letter from Wade Hampton at Millwood, dated May 8, 1896, to Major T. G. Barker. Marked 'Personal' in the upper left corner. Hampton writes that the veterans at Houston have put him in command of this department of 'Gordon's Army' and that he must travel to Richmond for the laying of the cornerstone of Mr. Davis' Monument. He wants his old staff with him — Barker as Chief of Staff — and notes that Dr. Taylor and Lewenden will also go. He jokes about Barker practicing horseback riding before directing military movements again. He explains he has been laid up by an inflammation in his leg and is on crutches, but hopes to be on his feet soon when he will go to Washington. The letter continues on the next scan.

people: Mrs. Northrop; Mrs. Walker; Mrs. Milliken; Auntie
notes: Continuation of the multi-page manuscript letter from pages 261–262. Written in brown ink in a more open hand than 262 (largely horizontal, not cross-hatched), making this page substantially more legible. The text is a deathbed scene: the dying woman tells Mrs. Northrop that she is willing to die and wishes to be baptised by the Priest; Mrs. Northrop and the Priest succeed in clearing the room; the dying woman declares 'I do it only on his account I am unchanged and die in the faith of my forefathers and you must say so'; Mr. Walker objects to her baptism but she takes his hand and turns her wasted face to him, saying 'I say it is only right to Christian as he thought I could not go to Heaven unless Baptised but it is only to make him happy'. The writer reports a long conversation with Mrs. Walker about being a Protestant, his trust in God's mercy for pardon, and concern for the children: 'Yes tell Mother that I have thought of this continually since she left & throughout my illness with intense affection; tell her that I thought of her in my dying moments, gave her my love.' There is a hole punch at the top center. Continuation of letter from pages 261–262.

notes: Typewritten letter on the printed letterhead of 'W. Huger FitzSimons / Law Offices of W. Huger FitzSimons / Charleston, S.C.' (with branch office at Aiken, S.C. embossed at upper left), dated November 5, 1918. Addressed to 'Miss Ellen B. FitzSimons, c/o Mr Gaillard B. FitzSimons, 545 South Church St., Spartanburg, S.C.' Discusses business returning from absence: a legal holiday with the bank closed, arrangements with Mr Pringle to draw interest as soon as possible, deposit of $5500 in the Bank of Charleston under date of October 28, 1918, mortgage signature requirements, the recording of the Mortgage at the Office of the Register of Deeds, Henderson County, N.C., in Book 46 page 310; affirms the Mr Osborne sale at $15,000, paid for via U.S. documentary stamps upon year deed, lawyer fee $11.10, and recounts a delightful visit to Gaillie's home in Spartanburg, with warm description of Gaillie's beautiful daughters and his own son's natural manners. Signed 'Yours aff'l'y / W Huger FitzSimons.' Some words and figures uncertain because of typewriter strikeover, the dim ribbon, and pencilled annotations along the right margin (apparently 'Gaillard's' [box?], 'Huger's letter,' and other notes whose exact wording is uncertain).

notes: Typewritten letter dated September 12th, 1918, addressed to 'Mr Thomas E. Osborne, Horse Shoe, N.C.' from W. Huger FitzSimons. Conveys the terms on which his sister (Ellen B. FitzSimons) is willing to sell her Mills River Farm — referring back to her letter of January 16th, 1917: fifteen thousand five hundred ($15,500.00) dollars for the Farm, not including the Mountain land which she wishes to reserve; $5000.00 cash and balance ($10,500.00) in five equal successive annual installments of $2100.00 each with interest on all unpaid credit portion at six (6%) per cent per annum payable annually; advises Mr Osborne to wait for sister's confirmation but expects to get the rent for this year. He had written to Santor (or Sauter) from Hendersonville and explains that he had promised to mention Mr Osborne and would shortly be in Charleston. Signed 'Sincerely yours,' (typed name not visible). Below the typescript a handwritten note in blue ink reads: 'Dear Sister: / Above is a copy of my letter / to Mr Osborne of this date. / [initial flourish — possibly 'H.J.G.' or similar] / Sept 12/18.' Several details in the typewritten body are uncertain because of strikeover and a faded ribbon.

The first sheet of a handwritten letter dated 'Oct 1st, 1918,' opening 'Dear Sister,'. The hand is loose cursive in brown ink on plain stationery; the page appears to have been mounted to the album near its upper edge with the lower edge free. Only the first sheet is present on this album page; the letter presumably continues on the next page. Sender unsigned on this sheet — context (date, references to 'home,' 'Dad,' and 'mother,' and overseas mail delays) is consistent with a WWI letter written from France.

This sheet continues a letter begun on page 342 (dated 'Oct 1st, 1918, Dear Sister') and signed 'Theodore' on page 344. The signature and earlier address suggest the writer is Theodore Barker FitzSimons writing from overseas service in the First World War; the addressee 'Dear Sister' is most likely Amy FitzSimons (later Mrs. James Pickens Walker), the album's compiler.

FitzSimons (the former Clara Mather of Savannah); the groom is the son of Mr. and Mrs. Frank Lockwood FitzSimons of Hendersonville (the former Margarita Kershaw). The body discusses the recent death of 'Eddie Rish,' his wife Em's adjustment to new bifocal eyeglasses, a Sunday visit at 'the children's' near a fruit-express upset at Scuffletown, weekend visitors from Ridgeland and Okeetee Club (including Mrs. Mary Bass, widow of former Okeetee superintendent Burton Bobby Bass), and an anecdote retold by Mrs. Bass about Christmas with the colored help and 'the FitzSimons.' notes: First sheet of a three-page letter on cream wove paper in blue-ink cursive, dated 'Tuesday' at the upper right and beginning 'Dear Mother.' Sam writes from home after a Sunday-night return from a visit to his mother in Panama City. He explains that Janice's father (referred to as 'J. P.' on the following sheets) has had heart trouble and the doctors have advised him to slow down; while Sam and Janice were visiting in Panama City, J. P. asked Sam if he would consider going to work for him with a view to eventually buying out the business. Pairs with pages 372 and 373.

people: Sam FitzSimons (Samuel Gaillard FitzSimons, III); Mary H. FitzSimons; Janice FitzSimons
notes: Second sheet of the letter begun on page 371, blue-ink cursive on cream paper. Sam continues with the details of his prospective new job with Janice's father: that it is to be straight business — he will work for one year and then, if both parties are satisfied, begin buying him out, an arrangement that may take 10 to 20 years; that Janice's sister is equally entitled to her share; that his salary will start around $10,000 a year; that he and Janice will be happier living in Panama City; and that he has told his current employer he is leaving in three weeks.

people: Sam FitzSimons (Samuel Gaillard FitzSimons, III); Mary H. FitzSimons
notes: Final sheet of the letter begun on page 371, blue-ink cursive on cream paper. Sam closes that his current employer (I. P., presumably International Paper) has said he would be welcome back if dissatisfied with the new arrangement and even asked him to consider staying on if he were transferred back to Panama City; that he still feels he must try being in business for himself; that he and Janice are happy about moving back to Panama City and closer to his mother; that they will move the week of the 24th. Signed 'Sam.'

The writer (apparently Mary, Sam's mother — companion to the page 369 letter to Amy in the same hand) reports that Sam, Janice and 'little Bo' have just spent a week with her; describes Bo's only word as 'cooky'; encloses a letter received from Sam the previous Friday in which he announced his decision (cf. pages 371–373) to leave his current employer for a heating and air-conditioning business; notes that Mr. Cooperath has had a heart murmur and intends to retire. The transcription below treats the heavily struck-through phrasing as a faithful first-draft text.
